fields_json: {"name":"Jenny Wilkins","bio":"Retired Head Librarian at Welles-Turner Memorial Library (32 years). Garden club member specializing in hostas, native ferns, and echinacea.","email":"jenny.wilkins@Finthesiss.ai"}
{"name":"Ruth Callahan","bio":"President of the Glastonbury Garden Club. Passionate advocate for native plantings and perennial borders in Connecticut.","email":"ruth.callahan.ct@gmail.com"}
{"name":"Linda Chu","bio":"Glastonbury Garden Club member with a specialty in shade gardening and woodland perennials.","email":"linda.chu.ct@gmail.com"}
{"name":"James Whitfield","bio":"Library volunteer at Welles-Turner Memorial Library and organizer of the quarterly used-book sale.","email":"james.whitfield.ct@gmail.com"}
{"name":"Peggy Doyle","bio":"Retired librarian and longtime book club co-organizer. Writes the books-received column for the garden club newsletter.","email":"peggy.doyle.ct@gmail.com"}
{"title":"Hostas","slug":"hostas"}

{"title":"Growing the Hosta June Cultivar in Connecticut Shade Gardens","slug":"hosta-june-cultivar-connecticut","body":"The hosta cultivar June is one of the showiest shade perennials you can grow in a Connecticut garden. Its chartreuse heart-shaped leaves, edged in blue-green, emerge in April and hold their color well into September. In my Connecticut garden I grow three large clumps along the north side of the house where they receive morning light and afternoon shade. Plant in moist, well-drained soil amended with compost. Divide every four to five years in early spring before the leaves fully unfurl. Slugs are the main pest; use copper tape or diatomaceous earth around the crown.","season":"Spring","tags":["hostas","shade-gardening","Connecticut","perennials"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-hosta-june"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Glastonbury Garden Club Spring Planting Guide 2024","slug":"spring-planting-guide-2024","body":"Each March the Glastonbury Garden Club compiles its spring planting guide for members. Start seeds indoors under grow lights in late February — tomatoes, basil, and annual flowers. Harden off seedlings over ten days in April before transplanting after last frost (typically around May 10 in Glastonbury). For perennial beds, top-dress with two inches of compost and divide any overcrowded clumps. Ruth Callahan recommends planting native echinacea in full sun with excellent drainage; Linda Chu favors adding a top-dressing of leaf mold to shade beds. Garden center hours at Glastonbury Garden Center (call Tom Braddock at 860-555-0411) extend through Memorial Day.","season":"Spring","tags":["spring-planting","Glastonbury","garden-club","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-spring-planting"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Caring for Native Ferns in Connecticut Shade Gardens","slug":"native-fern-care-connecticut","body":"Connecticut is home to several excellent native ferns suited to the shaded and part-shaded home garden. Cinnamon fern (Osmundastrum cinnamomeum) grows to four feet and tolerates wet feet; ostrich fern (Matteuccia struthiopteris) spreads by runners to form dramatic colonies; Christmas fern (Polystichum acrostichoides) stays evergreen through Zone 5 winters. Along the north fence in my Connecticut garden I combine all three with hostas and astilbe for a four-season display. Plant in early spring or late summer. Keep consistently moist the first season. In November mulch with three inches of shredded leaves to protect crowns from frost heave.","season":"Spring","tags":["native-plants","ferns","shade-gardening","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-native-ferns"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Echinacea in Three Colors: A Midsummer Border","slug":"echinacea-three-colors-border","body":"Growing echinacea in multiple colors transforms a sunny border from a single-note planting into a tapestry. In Jenny's south border in my Connecticut garden the purpurea species anchors the back, white coneflower (Echinacea purpurea 'Alba') softens the middle, and the warm Magnus cultivar bridges the two. All three are grown from divisions shared at the Glastonbury Garden Club spring plant swap. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil. Deadhead spent blooms to encourage reblooming, or leave seedheads for goldfinches in autumn. These plants are deer-resistant and drought-tolerant once established — ideal for Connecticut summers.","season":"Summer","tags":["echinacea","coneflower","native-plants","pollinators","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-ruth"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-echinacea-trio"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Black-Eyed Susans: Connecticut's Midsummer Workhorse","slug":"black-eyed-susan-midsummer","body":"Rudbeckia hirta and its cultivars are the backbone of the Connecticut midsummer border. They bloom reliably from July through September, tolerate clay soil with amendment, and self-sow gently without becoming invasive. Pair them with echinacea, ornamental grasses, and late-blooming salvia for a long-season native meadow effect. In my Connecticut garden Jenny grows the Goldsturm cultivar along the sunny front walk, where it blooms from late July until the first hard frost. Cut back by half in late May to promote bushier stems and delay bloom time for successive waves of color. Leave seedheads over winter for birds.","season":"Summer","tags":["rudbeckia","native-plants","pollinators","Connecticut","midsummer"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-black-eyed-susan"}},"published":true}

{"title":"How and When to Divide Hostas in New England","slug":"hosta-division-new-england","body":"Hostas rarely need dividing for health, but after five or six years a large clump benefits from being split to refresh vigor and to share with friends. The best time in Connecticut is early spring, when the spears are just emerging — usually the first two weeks of April in Glastonbury. Use a sharp spade to slice through the crown cleanly. Each division should have at least three to five growing points. Replant immediately at the same depth, water thoroughly, and mulch to retain moisture. Jenny shared divisions of the June cultivar at the Glastonbury Garden Club spring exchange in April 2025 with Ruth Callahan and Linda Chu.","season":"Spring","tags":["hostas","division","spring-planting","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-hosta-division"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Preparing Native Ferns for Winter in Connecticut","slug":"native-fern-fall-prep","body":"As temperatures drop below 50°F in October, it is time to begin putting native ferns to bed for the winter. Leave the fronds intact through November — they protect the crown and provide late-season habitat for beneficial insects. After a hard frost cut back deciduous species like cinnamon fern to within two inches of the ground. Christmas fern fronds lie flat in winter and can be left in place; they re-green in spring. Apply a three-inch layer of shredded leaves over all fern crowns before the ground freezes. Avoid bark mulch, which can mat and encourage rot. In early spring rake away the mulch gently as new fiddleheads emerge.","season":"Fall","tags":["native-plants","ferns","fall-prep","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-linda"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-fall-fern-care"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Designing a Connecticut Shade Garden with Hostas and Ferns","slug":"shade-garden-design-connecticut","body":"The north side of a New England cape cod offers some of the most rewarding planting opportunities: reliable moisture, protection from afternoon heat stress, and a canvas for bold foliage texture. Jenny's shade border in my Connecticut garden pairs the chartreuse Hosta June with the blue-leaved Halcyon and the giant Sum and Substance. Underplanted with ostrich fern and underscored with astilbe Fanal, the border looks full from May through October. Key principles: layer by height (tall ferns at back, medium hostas in middle, low astilbe in front), vary leaf texture (smooth hosta against lacy fern frond), and keep the soil consistently moist with a shredded-leaf mulch two inches deep.","season":"Spring","tags":["shade-gardening","hostas","ferns","design","Connecticut"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-shade-garden-map"}},"published":true}

{"title":"June Perennial Border Update: What's Blooming at Winding Brook Lane","slug":"perennial-border-june-update","body":"June is the month when the perennial border in my Connecticut garden reaches its first peak. The echinacea and rudbeckia are setting buds. Astilbe Fanal has gone from its first pink flush to its full rich red. The hostas have fully leafed out and the June cultivar is glowing chartreuse in the morning light. Ruth Callahan visited on June 10 to see the border and suggested adding a late-blooming blue salvia to bridge into August after the astilbe fades. Linda Chu brought a division of Japanese forest grass from her own garden, which Jenny has tucked into the front of the shade bed as a soft gold accent. Overall the border is performing beautifully this season.","season":"Summer","tags":["perennials","Connecticut","summer","garden-club","midsummer"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-perennial-border"}},"published":true}

{"title":"Harriet Wilkins's Botanical Watercolors of Connecticut Native Ferns","slug":"harriet-wilkins-botanical-watercolors","body":"Harriet Wilkins, retired art teacher and Jenny's spouse, has spent the past two summers painting botanical watercolors of Connecticut's native ferns. The series currently includes cinnamon fern, ostrich fern, and Christmas fern — all grown in my Connecticut garden. The paintings were displayed at the Glastonbury Community Center in September 2024 alongside an interpretive panel written by Jenny. Prints are available by contacting Harriet at harriet.wilkins.ct@gmail.com. Ruth Callahan has requested that a set be hung in the Glastonbury Garden Club meeting room.","season":"Fall","tags":["art","native-plants","ferns","Connecticut","community"],"contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-harriet-painting"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"Fall Plant Swap — October 17, 2026 at Glastonbury Community Center","slug":"fall-plant-swap-2026","body":"Mark your calendars! The Glastonbury Garden Club fall plant swap returns Saturday October 17, 2026 at 10 AM at the Glastonbury Community Center, 300 Welles Street. Bring divisions of healthy perennials, clearly labeled with plant name and light requirements, in sturdy pots or wrapped in damp newspaper. Jenny Wilkins will bring divisions of Hosta June and Connecticut native ferns. Ruth Callahan has confirmed she will bring echinacea and rudbeckia. Linda Chu is planning Japanese forest grass divisions. No invasives please. Contact Ruth Callahan at ruth.callahan.ct@gmail.com or Michael Egan at megan@glastonburycommunity.org for venue questions.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-ruth"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-plant-swap-2026"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"June 2026 Garden Update from Jenny Wilkins","slug":"newsletter-june-2026-garden-update","body":"Hello fellow gardeners — June has arrived and the borders in my Connecticut garden are at their best. The hosta June is glowing chartreuse in the morning light, the echinacea purpurea is setting buds, and the native ferns have fully unfurled their fronds along the north fence. This month's book club selection (July 5 at Glastonbury Library, 10 AM) is a lovely natural history title that Peggy found at the winter book sale. Ruth has asked all members to send in one paragraph about what is blooming in their garden right now for the July newsletter. Send to jenny.wilkins@Finthesiss.ai or ruth.callahan.ct@gmail.com by June 28.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-perennial-border"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"Spring 2025: Welcome to Another Garden Season in Glastonbury","slug":"newsletter-spring-2025-welcome","body":"Spring is here and the garden club is back in full swing. Our March meeting at Glastonbury Library drew 18 members. Ruth Callahan announced the spring plant exchange will be held at her home on April 12. Jenny Wilkins is preparing divisions of Hosta June, native Christmas fern, and cinnamon fern for the exchange. The spring book sale at Welles-Turner is April 26 — please drop off donations by April 20. Linda Chu shared a wonderful tip: applying a half-inch of worm castings around hostas in early April produces noticeably larger leaves by June. Details on the spring planting workshop (May 3, Glastonbury Community Center) are in the enclosed flyer.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-ruth"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-newsletter-banner"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"Fall 2025: Preparing Your Garden for a Connecticut Winter","slug":"newsletter-fall-2025-prep","body":"September signals the start of fall garden preparation in Connecticut. Jenny Wilkins recommends waiting until after the first hard frost to cut back most perennials, allowing seedheads to feed birds through October. Leave echinacea and rudbeckia seedheads standing — goldfinches love them through November. Begin dividing overgrown hostas now if you missed spring; plants have time to establish before freeze-up. The fall book sale at Welles-Turner is October 11 — a wonderful collection of British mysteries and literary fiction has come in this summer. Linda Chu reminds members to mulch all newly divided perennials before Thanksgiving with three inches of shredded leaves.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-jenny"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-fall-fern-care"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"Winter 2026: Seed Catalogs, Book Sale, and Planning for Spring","slug":"newsletter-winter-2026","body":"January in Glastonbury: the ground is frozen, the hostas are dormant, and the seed catalogs have arrived. Jenny Wilkins recommends ordering native plant seeds early — Connecticut Audubon's native seed mix and Prairie Moon Nursery's fern spore packets sell out by February. The winter book sale at Welles-Turner is January 31 (see full write-up by Peggy Doyle in this issue). Ruth Callahan has scheduled the first 2026 garden club meeting for February 14, 10 AM, at the Glastonbury Library meeting room. Topic: planning a native plant border from scratch. All members welcome; bring your garden plan if you have one.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-peggy"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-newsletter-banner"}},"published":true}

{"headline":"Plant Spotlight: Echinacea for Pollinators and Cut Flowers","slug":"newsletter-echinacea-spotlight","body":"This month Ruth Callahan puts the spotlight on echinacea — coneflower — as the ideal dual-purpose perennial for Connecticut sunny borders. Blooming from late June through September, echinacea draws bumblebees, swallowtails, and skippers throughout its season. The dried seedheads attract goldfinches well into December. As a cut flower the blooms last up to two weeks in a vase. Ruth grows the species purpurea alongside the Alba white cultivar and the warm-toned Magnus; Jenny Wilkins has added a pale yellow cultivar this season as a trial. Plant in full sun, well-drained soil. No deadheading needed if you want self-sown seedlings to fill gaps.","contributor":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Entry","id":"contributor-ruth"}},"heroImage":{"sys":{"type":"Link","linkType":"Asset","id":"asset-echinacea-closeup"}},"published":true}
